solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int strangePrinter(string s) {
        int n = s.length();
        if (n == 0) return 0;

        vector<vector<int>> dp(n, vector<int>(n, 0));

        for (int l = 1; l < n; ++l) {
            dp[l][l] = 1;
            for (int r = l + 1; r < n; ++r) {
                dp[l][r] = dp[l][r - 1] + 1;
                for (int k = l; k < r; ++k) {
                    if (s[k] == s[r]) {
                        if (dp[k + 1][r - 1] < dp[l][r]) {
                            dp[l][r] = dp[k + 1][r - 1] + 1;
                        }
                    }
                }
                if (s[l] == s[r]) {
                    if (dp[l][r - 1] < dp[l][r]) {
                        dp[l][r] = dp[l][r - 1];
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        return dp[0][n - 1];
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n^3)
space complexity: O(n^2)
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution uses dynamic programming to compute the minimum number of turns required to print the string. It builds a 2D DP table where `dp[l][r]` represents the minimum turns to print the substring `s[l..r]`. The key idea is to reduce the problem by considering overlapping subproblems and leveraging the fact that printing the same character in one turn can reduce the number of operations. The algorithm is efficient for the given constraint (n ≤ 100).
